title: Split, round 9: fast B off the pick, or stay on the default?

source.note: MOCK DATA — hand-authored fixture in HenrikDev match shape. A real deployment builds scenarios like this from the signed-in user's own recent matches via debrief-fetch-valorant-match; numbers below are realistic but not from a real match.

presented_to_user.role: You are the in-game leader on the attacking side, playing Sova.
presented_to_user.situation: Split, round 9, score 4–4, both teams on full buys. Your team is 40 seconds into a slow default: you hold mid-top with your Raze, your Omen and Jett are lurking A ramp, your Sage is with you. Your recon dart just found the CT mid player and your Raze converted — it's 5v4 with 45 seconds on the round clock. The dead player was the mid-connector defender, so the door between you and B site is currently uncontested. You believe B is now held by one defender plus whoever rotates. Your slow default was building toward a late A hit.
known_facts: 45 seconds left on the round clock; a B execute through mid takes roughly 15 seconds from your current positions, a spike plant about 5 more.
CT rotations from A to B on Split take 10–12 seconds through heaven; the defenders confirmed your mid presence when their player dropped.
Your utility: Sova dart used, shock darts up; Raze has satchels and grenade; Omen smokes both up (but he is A ramp, ~14 seconds from B); Sage wall and slows up, with you in mid.
The A-lurk pair (Omen, Jett) is currently applying pressure — two defenders are anchored A because of them.
Your team's planted-spike conversion this match: 3 wins in 4 plants. On rounds with no plant you are 1 for 3.
The B site defender is a Cypher who has been playing retake setups all half, not contact holds.
presented_to_user.question: Do you call the fast B hit off the pick, or keep developing the slow A default you planned? Explain your call — what are you weighing, what are you betting on, and what would make you wrong?

ground_truth.actual_decision: The team called the fast B hit immediately: Sage walled the heaven rotation line, Raze satcheled onto site, and the spike was down 21 seconds after the mid pick with a 5v4 post-plant.
ground_truth.actual_outcome: The fast B call won the round. The Cypher fell back to a retake setup without contesting the hit (as his half-long pattern suggested), and the A-anchored defenders arrived 4 seconds after the plant into a set 5v4 post-plant crossfire — the retake died 3v5. The 10–12 second heaven rotation was exactly the window the call exploited: hitting inside it meant taking a 1v4 site fight instead of a 3v5 one. Post-round, the slow-default line was modeled at roughly 45% (late A hit into two anchored defenders plus rotations, no plant in 1 of 3 attempts this match); the fast B line at roughly 70% (5v4, uncontested mid door, retake-passive anchor, 3-of-4 post-plant conversion).
decision_rationale_factors: Man-advantage windows decay: 5v4 is worth the most in the 10–12 seconds before the defense re-anchors. The value of the pick is not the number, it's the window — and windows close.

The pick was positional, not just numerical: the dead player was the mid-connector defender, which converts mid control into a free approach to B. A random pick elsewhere would not justify the same tempo change.

The team's own conversion stats pointed at playing for the plant: 3-of-4 with spike down versus 1-of-3 without. The fast hit maximizes plant probability; the slow default risks another no-plant round.

Utility check before tempo check: Sage wall for the heaven rotation line and Raze site-clear utility were both up and both mid — the two agents the hit needs were already in position. The A-lurk pair did not need to arrive for the plant, only for the post-plant.
The clock: 45 seconds fits hit-plus-post-plant comfortably; it does NOT comfortably fit continuing the default, re-grouping, and still having spike-down time if the late A hit stalls.
ground_truth.defensible_alternative: Staying on the slow A default is defensible IF it is argued from the A-side investment: two defenders were anchored on the lurk pair, and a coordinated 5-man A hit off that pressure — using the man advantage where your utility ISN'T split — is a real line. A user who keeps the default but names the cost (the mid window closing, the rotation re-anchoring), commits to hitting A before the clock forces a rushed plant, and prices the no-plant risk their own 1-of-3 stat carries, is showing sound process with a preference for the prepared hit over the improvised one. 'Stick to the plan' with no engagement with what the pick changed is where it stops being defensible.
common_mistakes: Treating the pick as just a number (5v4) rather than a positional fact — the mid-connector door being open is what makes B fast, not the man count.
Ignoring the window: calling the B hit but 'after we regroup', which arrives exactly when the 10–12 second rotation has re-anchored the site.
Vibes-only tempo ('we're up a man, go go go') with no utility check — hitting a site with the Omen smokes 14 seconds away and no plan for heaven.
Ignoring the team's own plant-conversion numbers sitting in the facts.
No falsifier — never naming what would keep them on the default (e.g. the dead defender being a flex player whose B anchor is still home).

verified_data_points.note: MOCK values, internally consistent, in the shape a HenrikDev round payload supports.

window: the decaying man-advantage window against the heaven rotation
position: what the specific pick opened positionally
utility: the utility state and who is actually in position
clock: the clock math for the hit versus the default

missing_priority: the window — a 5v4 is worth the most in the 10–12 seconds before the defense re-anchors
what the pick actually opened: the dead mid-connector defender is a free door to B, not just a number
your own numbers in the facts — 3-of-4 with the spike down, 1-of-3 without
the utility check: Sage and Raze are mid and ready; the Omen smokes are 14 seconds away
an explicit falsifier — what would keep you on the default
key_insights: The value of a pick is the window it opens, not the number it changes — hitting inside the 10–12 second rotation meant a 1v4 site fight instead of a 3v5 retake.
The strongest evidence was already yours: 3-of-4 with the spike planted, 1-of-3 without. The fast hit is the play-for-the-plant call.
Tempo calls are utility checks first: the hit worked because the wall and the site-clear were already mid — the same call with the smokes 14 seconds away is a different call.
mock_synth.covering_decision_fb: Clear call to stay on the default — the opposite of the round-winning call, but committed and coherent. Capped slightly because the pick specifically opened B and the advantage window was decaying while the default developed.
mock_synth.matched_decision_fb: Clear call to convert the pick into the fast B hit — changing tempo the moment the situation changes is exactly the committed, evidence-led call this dimension rewards.
mock_synth.risk_hint: the Cypher contact-holding for once instead of falling back, or the B hit arriving just after the heaven rotation beats it
